compiled_state.title: As a developer, ticket close completes in under 10 seconds with 200 non-archived tickets
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Description

**What**: Create a benchmark test that validates the performance target: closing a ticket with 200 non-archived tickets in the tracker completes in under 10 seconds.
**Why**: The epic's primary goal is eliminating timeouts (2+ minutes). This story provides the measurable validation signal that the optimization work in Stories 1-3 achieved the target.
**Scope**:
- IN: Benchmark test with realistic ticket population (mix of types, statuses, dependencies, archived tickets); wall-clock time measurement of the full ticket transition close path
- OUT: Further optimization work — this story validates, it does not fix

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, a benchmark test confirms that ticket transition ... closed completes in under 10 seconds with 200 non-archived tickets in the tracker
  ← Satisfies: "Closing a ticket with 200 non-archived tickets in the tracker completes in under 10 seconds"
- When this story is complete, the benchmark test creates a realistic ticket population (mix of types, statuses, dependencies) and measures wall-clock time
  ← Satisfies: "Closing a ticket with 200 non-archived tickets in the tracker completes in under 10 seconds"
- When this story is complete, the benchmark measures the full ticket transition close path including the ticket-unblock.py subprocess call, not just the consolidated batch scan — the 10-second target applies to the entire user-observable wall-clock time
  ← Satisfies: "Closing a ticket with 200 non-archived tickets in the tracker completes in under 10 seconds"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Testing] Test with archived tickets present alongside non-archived to verify exclusion actually reduces scan time

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Escalate to the user whenever you do not have high confidence in your understanding of the work, approach, or intent. "High confidence" means clear evidence from the codebase or ticket context — not inference or reasonable assumption. When in doubt, stop and ask rather than guess.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Story complete: benchmark test confirms ticket close under 10s with 200 non-archived tickets. Actual close time: ~0.5s.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
